full_context: Excitation system nominal response $V_E$ is defined as the ratio of the increment of the excitation system output voltage determined from the voltage response curve to the rated field voltage (see Figure 2). Assuming this ratio remains constant, the expanded voltage–time area equals the actual area obtained in the first 0.5-second interval. Formula: $V_E = \frac{\Delta U_E}{0.5 U_{fN}} (s^{-1})$. It is determined with the excitation system loaded by a resistance equal to $U_f/I_N$ and a sufficiently inductive load, taking into account voltage variations and the effects of current and voltage waveforms.

variable_definitions.name: Excitation system nominal response
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Index describing the dynamic response speed of the excitation system

variable_definitions.name: Increment of excitation output voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Change in excitation system output voltage during the first 0.5 s

variable_definitions.name: Rated field voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: DC voltage at the field winding terminals under rated operating condition (see Section 2.8)

task_description: 1. Obtain \(\Delta U_E\) (output voltage change within 0.5 s); 2. Obtain \(U_{fN}\) (rated field voltage); 3. Compute denominator \(0.5\times U_{fN}\); 4. Compute \(V_E=\Delta U_E/(0.5\times U_{fN})\); 5. Output the nominal response value

full_context: Static-voltage error ratio is defined as: with the load-current compensator removed, and prime-mover speed and power factor varying within specified ranges, the rate of change of generator terminal voltage when the generator load changes from rated to zero. Formula: $\epsilon = \frac{U_0 - U_N}{U_N} \times 100\%$. Here $\epsilon$ is the static-voltage error ratio (percent), $U_N$ is the generator terminal voltage under rated load (volts), and $U_0$ is the terminal voltage at no load (volts). This index reflects the static characteristic of terminal voltage versus load.

variable_definitions.name: Static-voltage error ratio
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Voltage change rate caused by load variation

variable_definitions.name: No-load terminal voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Generator terminal voltage at no-load condition

variable_definitions.name: Rated terminal voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Generator terminal voltage under rated load

task_description: 1. Measure no-load voltage \(U_0\); 2. Obtain rated voltage \(U_N\); 3. Compute voltage difference \(U_0-U_N\); 4. Compute ratio \((U_0-U_N)/U_N\); 5. Multiply by 100% to get percentage; 6. Output \(\epsilon\)

full_context: Voltage compensative ratio is defined as: when the generator power factor equals zero, as reactive current increases from zero to the rated stator current, the change rate of the generator terminal voltage. Formula: $D = \frac{U_0 - U}{U_N} \times 100\%$. Here $D$ is the voltage compensative ratio (percent), $U_0$ is the no-load terminal voltage (volts), $U$ is the terminal voltage when power factor equals zero and reactive current equals the rated stator current (volts), and $U_N$ is the rated voltage (volts). The compensative ratio with the load-current compensator removed is called the natural voltage compensative ratio $D_0$.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage compensative ratio
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Voltage change rate caused by reactive current variation

variable_definitions.name: No-load terminal voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Generator terminal voltage at no load

variable_definitions.name: Terminal voltage at rated reactive current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Terminal voltage when reactive current equals rated stator current and PF=0

variable_definitions.name: Rated terminal voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Terminal voltage under rated load

task_description: 1. Measure no-load voltage \(U_0\); 2. Measure terminal voltage \(U\) at rated reactive current; 3. Obtain rated voltage \(U_N\); 4. Compute voltage difference \(U_0-U\); 5. Compute ratio \((U_0-U)/U_N\); 6. Multiply by 100% to get percentage; 7. Output \(D\)

full_context: Reactive current compensator ratio is defined as the increment of voltage adjustment ratio caused by energizing the reactive current compensator. Formula: $K_{RCC} = D - D_0$. Here $K_{RCC}$ is the compensator ratio (percent), $D$ is the voltage compensative ratio with the compensator in service, and $D_0$ is the natural voltage compensative ratio (compensator out of service). In engineering applications, when the static-voltage error ratio is less than 1%, $D_0$ may be regarded as zero, i.e., $K_{RCC} = D$.

variable_definitions.name: Reactive current compensator ratio
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Improvement in voltage compensative ratio due to the compensator

variable_definitions.name: Voltage compensative ratio
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Compensative ratio with compensator in service (see Section 2.21)

variable_definitions.name: Natural voltage compensative ratio
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Compensative ratio with compensator out of service

task_description: 1. Measure compensative ratio \(D\) with compensator; 2. Measure natural compensative ratio \(D_0\); 3. Compute \(K_{RCC}=D-D_0\); 4. Output the compensator ratio

full_context: For a generator step response at no load and rated operating condition, the overshoot $M_p$ is defined as: the percentage ratio of the difference between the maximum terminal voltage $U_m$ and the steady-state terminal voltage $U_{o2}$ to the steady-state change in terminal voltage $(U_{o2}-U_{o1})$, where $U_{o1}$ is the initial steady value and $U_{o2}$ is the final steady value. Formula: $M_p = \frac{U_m - U_{o2}}{U_{o2} - U_{o1}} \times 100\%$.

variable_definitions.name: Overshoot
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Ratio of maximum deviation during step response to steady change

variable_definitions.name: Maximum terminal voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Peak terminal voltage during the response

variable_definitions.name: Post-step steady value
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Steady terminal voltage after the step

variable_definitions.name: Pre-step steady value
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Initial terminal voltage before the step

potential_questions: In a step test with $U_{o1}=10000V$, $U_{o2}=10500V$, $U_m=11000V$, compute $M_p$
task_description: 1. Record pre-step voltage \(U_{o1}\); 2. Record post-step steady voltage \(U_{o2}\); 3. Measure peak voltage \(U_m\) during the response; 4. Compute numerator \(U_m-U_{o2}\); 5. Compute denominator \(U_{o2}-U_{o1}\); 6. Compute \(\frac{U_m-U_{o2}}{U_{o2}-U_{o1}}\); 7. Multiply by 100% to get \(M_p\)

full_context: Rectifier regulation characteristic: in a three-phase full-wave bridge circuit, the output voltage decreases nonlinearly as load current increases. The rectifier load factor $X_E$ is calculated by: $X_E = \frac{3\sqrt{3} X_\tau I_{fB}}{\pi U_{fB}}$. Here $X_\tau$ is the commutation reactance, $I_{fB}$ is the generator field current base value (Appendix A: field current required on the air-gap line to produce rated voltage under open-circuit characteristic), and $U_{fB}$ is the generator field voltage base value (Appendix A: product of $I_{fB}$ and the field-winding resistance base value).

variable_definitions.name: Rectifier load factor
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Parameter indicating the effect of commutation reactance on output voltage

variable_definitions.name: Commutation reactance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Internal inductive reactance of the rectifier AC source

variable_definitions.name: Field current base value
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Field current on the air-gap line producing rated voltage (Appendix A)

variable_definitions.name: Field voltage base value
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Product of $I_{fB}$ and field-winding resistance base value (Appendix A)

task_description: 1. Determine commutation reactance \(X_\tau\); 2. Obtain field current base \(I_{fB}\); 3. Obtain field voltage base \(U_{fB}\); 4. Compute constant \(3\sqrt{3}/\pi \approx 1.654\); 5. Compute numerator \(1.654\times X_\tau\times I_{fB}\); 6. Divide by \(U_{fB}\) to get \(X_E\)

full_context: For static excitation power units, the rectifier load factor $X_p$ is: $X_p = \frac{3 X_\tau I_{fB}}{\pi U_{fB}}$. Applicable to voltage-source excitation systems using three-phase full-controlled bridge rectifiers. Variables are as defined in Appendix B: $X_\tau$ is commutation reactance, $I_{fB}$ is field current base value, and $U_{fB}$ is field voltage base value.

variable_definitions.name: Rectifier load factor
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Commutation reactance influence parameter for static excitation

variable_definitions.name: Commutation reactance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Internal inductive reactance of the rectifier AC source

variable_definitions.name: Field current base value
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Field current on the air-gap line producing rated voltage (Appendix A)

variable_definitions.name: Field voltage base value
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Product of $I_{fB}$ and field-winding resistance base value (Appendix A)

task_description: 1. Determine commutation reactance \(X_\tau\); 2. Obtain field current base \(I_{fB}\); 3. Obtain field voltage base \(U_{fB}\); 4. Compute constant \(3/\pi \approx 0.955\); 5. Compute numerator \(0.955\times X_\tau\times I_{fB}\); 6. Divide by \(U_{fB}\) to get \(X_p\)

full_context: The exciter saturation function $S_E$ reflects the increase in excitation due to exciter saturation. Depending on the model, two forms are used: for models that do not separately simulate load effects, $S_E = \frac{A - B}{B}$; for models that simulate load effects, $S_E = \frac{C - B}{B}$. Here $A$ is the exciter field current on the constant-resistance load saturation curve at the corresponding output-voltage point, $B$ is the exciter field current on the air-gap line at the corresponding output-voltage point, and $C$ is the exciter field current on the open-circuit saturation curve at the corresponding output-voltage point.

variable_definitions.name: Saturation function
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Dimensionless parameter characterizing exciter saturation level

variable_definitions.name: Load saturation-curve current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Field current at the point on the constant-resistance load saturation curve

variable_definitions.name: Air-gap line current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Field current at the point on the air-gap line

variable_definitions.name: Open-circuit saturation-curve current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Field current at the point on the open-circuit saturation curve

potential_questions: Given $B=200A$ and $A=240A$ at a certain output voltage, compute $S_E$ for the non-load-effect model
task_description: 1. Choose the applicable model type; 2. Obtain air-gap line current \(B\); 3. Obtain \(A\) (load curve) or \(C\) (open-circuit curve) per model; 4. Compute difference \(A-B\) or \(C-B\); 5. Divide by \(B\) to get \(S_E\)

formula: \text{Voltage deviation}(\%) = \frac{\text{Measured voltage} - \text{System nominal voltage}}{\text{System nominal voltage}} \times 100\%
full_context: The basic measurement time window for voltage RMS should be 10 cycles, with adjacent windows contiguous but non-overlapping. Continuous measurement and averaging of RMS values yields the supply-voltage deviation. The calculation is: Voltage deviation (%) = (Measured voltage − System nominal voltage) / System nominal voltage × 100%. This formula computes the percentage deviation of supply voltage from nominal, for power quality monitoring.

variable_definitions.name: Measured voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Actual measured RMS averaged over a 10-cycle window

variable_definitions.name: System nominal voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Given nominal system voltage (GB/T156-2007, definition 3.1)

potential_questions: Given nominal voltage 220V and measured 215V, compute voltage deviation
task_description: 1. Obtain \(U_{\text{meas}}\) (measured voltage); 2. Obtain \(U_{\text{nom}}\) (system nominal voltage); 3. Compute difference \(U_{\text{meas}}-U_{\text{nom}}\); 4. Divide by \(U_{\text{nom}}\); 5. Multiply by 100% to get deviation

full_context: The monitored supply point is the monitoring point. By statistics of voltage deviation, the voltage compliance rate is obtained. The statistical time unit is minutes; total time may be month/week/season/year, and cumulative over-limit time is the sum of intervals where deviation exceeds limits. Calculation: Voltage compliance rate (%) = (1 − Over-limit time / Total time) × 100%. This evaluates voltage quality compliance.

variable_definitions.name: Over-limit time
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Cumulative time when voltage deviation exceeds limits

variable_definitions.name: Total time
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Total statistical operating time

potential_questions: For monthly total time 43200 min and over-limit 120 min, compute compliance rate
task_description: 1. Sum over-limit time \(t_{\text{over}}\); 2. Determine total time \(t_{\text{tot}}\); 3. Compute \(t_{\text{over}}/t_{\text{tot}}\); 4. Compute \(1- t_{\text{over}}/t_{\text{tot}}\); 5. Multiply by 100% to get compliance rate

full_context: The voltage compliance rate for each class of monitoring points is the average over the corresponding number of points: Monthly voltage compliance (%) = Σ(monitoring-point compliance)/n, where n is the number of monitoring points of that class. This computes the regional grid monthly voltage compliance statistic.

variable_definitions.name: Compliance of point i
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Voltage compliance rate of a single monitoring point

variable_definitions.name: Number of points
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Count of monitoring points

potential_questions: Three monitoring-point compliances are 98.5%/99.0%/97.8%; compute the monthly compliance
task_description: 1. Obtain each monitoring-point compliance \(\text{Compliance}_i\); 2. Sum all compliances \(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\text{Compliance}_i\); 3. Divide by number of points \(n\); 4. Get monthly compliance

full_context: Comprehensive voltage compliance for grid (annual/seasonal/monthly) $\gamma(\%) = 0.5\gamma_A + 0.5((\gamma_B+\gamma_C+\gamma_D)/3)$, where $\gamma_A$, $\gamma_B$, $\gamma_C$, $\gamma_D$ are the compliances for A/B/C/D classes of monitoring points. This computes comprehensive compliance with weighting by class.

variable_definitions.name: Class A compliance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Compliance at substations and plant buses supplying regional loads

variable_definitions.name: Class B compliance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Compliance for dedicated lines at 20 kV and above

variable_definitions.name: Class C compliance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Compliance for non-dedicated 10(6) kV supply

variable_definitions.name: Class D compliance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Compliance for 380/220 V low-voltage networks

task_description: 1. Obtain \(\gamma_A\), \(\gamma_B\), \(\gamma_C\), \(\gamma_D\); 2. Compute \(\gamma_B+\gamma_C+\gamma_D\); 3. Divide by 3; 4. Compute \(0.5\times\gamma_A\); 5. Compute \(0.5\times\frac{\gamma_B+\gamma_C+\gamma_D}{3}\); 6. Sum to get \(\gamma\)

full_context: Long-term flicker value caused solely by a fluctuating load: $P_{lt2} = (P_{lt1}^3 - P_{lt0}^3)^{1/3}$. Here $P_{lt1}$ is the measured long-term flicker with the fluctuating load in service, and $P_{lt0}$ is the background long-term flicker with the load out of service. This separates the flicker contribution of the fluctuating load.

variable_definitions.name: Flicker with load in
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Measured long-term flicker when the fluctuating load is in service

variable_definitions.name: Background flicker
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Measured long-term flicker with the fluctuating load out of service

variable_definitions.name: Load-only flicker
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Long-term flicker caused only by the fluctuating load

task_description: 1. Measure \(P_{lt1}\); 2. Measure \(P_{lt0}\); 3. Compute \(P_{lt1}^3\); 4. Compute \(P_{lt0}^3\); 5. Take the cubic difference \(P_{lt1}^3-P_{lt0}^3\); 6. Cube-root to obtain \(P_{lt2}=\sqrt[3]{P_{lt1}^3-P_{lt0}^3}\)

full_context: Total allowable flicker at the PCC point for all connected loads: $G = (L_P^3 - T^3 L_H^3)^{1/3}$. Here $L_P$ is the long-term flicker limit for the current voltage level, $L_H$ is the long-term limit for the higher voltage level, and $T$ is the flicker transfer coefficient (recommended 0.8). This determines the allowable total flicker at the PCC.

variable_definitions.name: PCC flicker limit
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Long-term flicker limit at the current voltage level

variable_definitions.name: Higher-level flicker limit
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Long-term flicker limit at the next higher voltage level

variable_definitions.name: Transfer coefficient
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Flicker transfer coefficient from higher to current level

task_description: 1. Obtain \(L_P\), \(L_H\), \(T\); 2. Compute \(L_P^3\); 3. Compute \(T^3\); 4. Compute \(L_H^3\); 5. Compute \(T^3\times L_H^3\); 6. Take cubic difference \(L_P^3-T^3L_H^3\); 7. Cube-root to obtain \(G=\sqrt[3]{L_P^3-T^3L_H^3}\)

full_context: Flicker limit for a single user: $E_i = G \times (S_i/S_t)^{1/3} \times (1/F)$. Here $S_i$ is the user's contractual power capacity, $S_t$ is the total supply capacity at the PCC, and $F$ is the simultaneity factor for fluctuating loads (0.2–0.3). The limit is apportioned by capacity proportion.

variable_definitions.name: Total flicker limit
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Allowable total long-term flicker at the PCC

variable_definitions.name: User capacity
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: User contractual capacity ($S_i=P_i/\cos\varphi_i$)

variable_definitions.name: Total supply capacity
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Total supply capacity at the PCC

variable_definitions.name: Simultaneity factor
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Simultaneity rate of fluctuating loads

task_description: 1. Obtain \(G\), \(S_i\), \(S_t\), \(F\); 2. Compute \(S_i/S_t\); 3. Take cube root \(\sqrt[3]{S_i/S_t}\); 4. Multiply by \(G\); 5. Multiply by \(1/F\); 6. Obtain \(E_i\)

full_context: Long-term flicker is computed from short-term flickers: $P_{lt} = \left( \sum P_{stj}^3 / 12 ight)^{1/3}$, where $P_{stj}$ is the $j$-th 10-minute short-term flicker within a 2-hour span (12 values). This converts multiple short-term flicker values into a long-term assessment.

variable_definitions.name: Short-term flicker
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: The $j$-th 10-minute short-term flicker value

variable_definitions.name: Long-term flicker
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: 2-hour long-term flicker value

task_description: 1. Obtain 12 \(P_{stj}\) values; 2. Compute each \(P_{stj}^3\); 3. Sum the 12 cubes \(\sum_{j=1}^{12} P_{stj}^3\); 4. Divide by 12; 5. Cube-root to obtain \(P_{lt}\)

full_context: For periodic rectangular voltage fluctuations, the short-term flicker is $P_{st} = d / d_{Lim}$. Here $d$ is the voltage change, and $d_{Lim}$ is the unit flicker voltage change read from the $P_{st}=1$ curve (Figure 1 / Table 4). This estimates flicker impact of regular fluctuating loads.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage change
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Percent change of voltage between adjacent extrema relative to nominal

variable_definitions.name: Unit flicker change
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Voltage change corresponding to $P_{st}=1$

variable_definitions.name: Short-term flicker
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Short-term flicker statistic

task_description: 1. Determine voltage change \(d\); 2. From frequency \(r\) read \(d_{Lim}\) from Table 4; 3. Compute \(P_{st}=d/d_{Lim}\)

full_context: Flicker compliance is the percentage of cumulative operating time within the flicker compliance range relative to the total statistical operating time. At a monitoring point, flicker compliance is computed as: Flicker compliance (%) = (1 − Over-limit time / Total time) × 100%. This evaluates flicker compliance level.

variable_definitions.name: Over-limit flicker time
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Cumulative time when long-term flicker exceeds the limit

variable_definitions.name: Total time
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Total statistical operating time

potential_questions: Given weekly total time 10080 min and over-limit 360 min, compute compliance
task_description: 1. Sum over-limit flicker time \(t_{\text{over}}\); 2. Determine total time \(t_{\text{tot}}\); 3. Compute \(t_{\text{over}}/t_{\text{tot}}\); 4. Compute \(1 - t_{\text{over}}/t_{\text{tot}}\); 5. Multiply by 100% to get compliance

full_context: Frequency correction of radio interference limits for high-voltage AC overhead transmission lines can be computed by: $ΔE = 5[1 - 2(\lg f)^2]$. This gives the interference field strength increment relative to 0.5 MHz, applicable to the 0.15–4 MHz band.

variable_definitions.name: Interference field increment
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Change in interference field strength relative to 0.5 MHz

variable_definitions.name: Frequency
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Measurement frequency for radio interference

task_description: 1. Determine measurement frequency \(f\); 2. Compute \(\lg f\); 3. Compute \((\lg f)^2\); 4. Compute \(2(\lg f)^2\); 5. Compute \(1 - 2(\lg f)^2\); 6. Compute \(\Delta E=5[1 - 2(\lg f)^2]\); 7. Output \(\Delta E\)

full_context: Frequency correction of radio interference limits can also be computed by $ΔE = 20\lg\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5 + f^{1.75}}\right) - 5$. This alternative expression applies to the 0.15–30 MHz band.

variable_definitions.name: Interference field increment
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Change in interference field strength relative to 0.5 MHz

variable_definitions.name: Frequency
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Measurement frequency for radio interference

task_description: 1. Determine measurement frequency \(f\); 2. Compute \(f^{1.75}\); 3. Compute \(0.5 + f^{1.75}\); 4. Compute \(1.5/(0.5 + f^{1.75})\); 5. Compute \(20\lg\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5 + f^{1.75}}\right)\); 6. Compute \(\Delta E = 20\lg\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5 + f^{1.75}}\right) - 5\); 7. Output \(\Delta E\)

full_context: Distance characteristic of radio interference for high-voltage AC overhead lines: $E_x = E + k\cdot\lg\left[\frac{400 + (H - h)^2}{X^2 + (H - h)^2}\right]$. This computes the interference field strength at $X$ meters from the projection of the edge conductor, applicable when distance is less than 100 m.

variable_definitions.name: Interference at distance X
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Radio interference field strength at $X$ m from edge conductor projection

variable_definitions.name: Interference at 20 m
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Radio interference field strength at 20 m from edge conductor projection

variable_definitions.name: Attenuation coefficient
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Coefficient of interference attenuation with distance

variable_definitions.name: Edge conductor height
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Ground clearance of the edge conductor at measurement point

variable_definitions.name: Antenna height
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Measurement antenna mounting height

variable_definitions.name: Measurement distance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Distance from edge conductor projection

task_description: 1. Determine base field \(E\); 2. Determine coefficient \(k\); 3. Measure edge conductor height \(H\); 4. Measure antenna height \(h\); 5. Determine distance \(X\); 6. Compute \((H-h)\); 7. Compute \((H-h)^2\); 8. Compute \(400 + (H-h)^2\); 9. Compute \(X^2 + (H-h)^2\); 10. Compute the ratio \(\frac{400 + (H-h)^2}{X^2 + (H-h)^2}\); 11. Compute \(\lg\) of the ratio; 12. Multiply by \(k\); 13. Compute \(E_x = E + k\cdot\lg(\cdots)\); 14. Output \(E_x\)

full_context: At 0.5 MHz, the radio interference field strength of high-voltage AC overhead lines can be computed by $E = 3.5g_{max} + 12r - 30 + 33\lg(20/D)$. This basic formula estimates the 50% value of radio interference field strength under fair-weather conditions.

variable_definitions.name: Radio interference field strength
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Field strength at 0.5 MHz

variable_definitions.name: Maximum surface potential gradient
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Maximum electric field strength at conductor surface

variable_definitions.name: Conductor radius
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Geometric radius of the conductor

variable_definitions.name: Direct distance
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Direct distance from interference point to conductor

task_description: 1. Measure \(g_{max}\); 2. Measure \(r\); 3. Determine \(D\); 4. Compute \(3.5g_{max}\); 5. Compute \(12r\); 6. Compute \(33\lg(20/D)\); 7. Compute \(E\) by the formula; 8. Output \(E\)

full_context: When no single phase of the three-phase conductors has an interference field strength exceeding any other phase by more than 3 dB(μV/m), the combined radio interference field strength is $E = (E_1 + E_2)/2 + 1.5$.

variable_definitions.name: Combined radio interference field
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Radio interference field strength after combining three phases

variable_definitions.name: Maximum interference
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Largest radio interference field among phases

variable_definitions.name: Second-largest interference
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Second-largest radio interference field among phases

task_description: 1. Measure \(E_1\) and \(E_2\); 2. Compute \(E_1+E_2\); 3. Divide by 2; 4. Add 1.5 to obtain \(E\); 5. Output \(E\)

full_context: Relative thermal aging rate of transformers: $V = \frac{\text{aging rate at } \theta_h}{\text{aging rate at } \theta_c} = 2^{\frac{\theta_h - \theta_c}{\theta_d}}$. This computes the relative rate when hotspot temperature changes, applicable to oil-immersed and dry-type transformers. $\theta_h$ is actual hotspot temperature; $\theta_c$ is rated hotspot temperature; $\theta_d$ is the life-loss doubling interval (6℃ for oil-immersed, 10℃ for dry-type).

variable_definitions.name: Actual hotspot temperature
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Maximum temperature reached by transformer windings

variable_definitions.name: Rated hotspot temperature
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Continuous operating hotspot temperature allowed by design

variable_definitions.name: Life-loss doubling interval
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Temperature rise causing doubling of aging rate (6℃ oil-immersed; 10℃ dry-type)

task_description: 1. Measure \(\theta_h\); 2. Determine \(\theta_c\); 3. Select \(\theta_d\) (6 for oil-immersed, 10 for dry-type); 4. Compute \(\theta_h-\theta_c\); 5. Compute \((\theta_h-\theta_c)/\theta_d\); 6. Compute \(2^{\frac{\theta_h-\theta_c}{\theta_d}}\); 7. Output \(V\)

full_context: When the grid-connection point voltage of a wind farm is between 20% and 90% of nominal, the wind farm shall inject dynamic reactive current to support voltage recovery: $I_T \ge 1.5 \times (0.9 - U_T) I_N$ ($0.2 \le U_T \le 0.9$). $U_T$ is the per-unit voltage at the connection point; $I_N$ is the rated current.

variable_definitions.name: Per-unit voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Voltage at grid-connection point relative to nominal

variable_definitions.name: Dynamic reactive current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Reactive current injected by the wind farm

variable_definitions.name: Rated current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Output current under rated conditions

task_description: 1. Determine \(I_N\); 2. Measure \(U_T\); 3. Compute \((0.9-U_T)\); 4. Compute \(1.5\times(0.9-U_T)\); 5. Compute \(I_{T,\min}=1.5\times(0.9-U_T)\times I_N\); 6. Output \(I_{T,\min}\)

full_context: When the grid-connection point voltage of a PV power plant is between 0.2 pu and 0.9 pu, dynamic reactive current shall be injected: $I_T \ge 1.5 \times (0.9 - U_T) I_N$. $U_T$ is the per-unit voltage; $I_N = \frac{\text{Rated capacity}}{\sqrt{3} \times \text{Rated voltage at connection}}$.

variable_definitions.name: Per-unit voltage
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Voltage at grid-connection point relative to nominal

variable_definitions.name: Dynamic reactive current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Reactive current injected by the PV plant

variable_definitions.name: Rated current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Output current under rated conditions computed from capacity and voltage

potential_questions: Given rated capacity 50 MW, rated connection voltage 35 kV, and $U_T=0.4$, compute minimum $I_T$
task_description: 1. Compute \(I_N = \frac{\text{Rated capacity}}{\sqrt{3}\times \text{Rated voltage}}\); 2. Measure \(U_T\); 3. Compute \((0.9-U_T)\); 4. Compute \(1.5\times(0.9-U_T)\); 5. Compute \(I_{T,\min}=1.5\times(0.9-U_T)\times I_N\); 6. Output \(I_{T,\min}\)

full_context: When the grid-connection point voltage of a PV power plant exceeds 0.9 pu, it shall satisfy $I_T=0$. This complements the dynamic reactive current rule to form a complete voltage support strategy.

variable_definitions.name: Dynamic reactive current
variable_definitions.physical_meaning: Reactive current injected by the PV plant

task_description: 1. Confirm \(U_T>0.9\); 2. Output \(I_T=0\)
